[48:44] before type 2 diabetes develops

[65:34] Not quite getting to 90 or 95 96 often, but it's right before that
[65:42] That could be
[65:45] Telling don't trust office values. Remember when patients are coming into your office for medical visits
[65:52] They're already nervous
[65:54] We intimidate people whether we intend to or not
[65:58] Doctors have more of the problem than nurse practitioners for sure
[66:02] But most of your patients are still a little bit intimidated by us because we're the smart professional
[66:08] And we have we hold their help in our hands and they are nervous when they come to see us
[66:14] And even when they're not
[66:16] They had to get the kids off to school. They had to make sure the kids had lunch the kids got their medicine
[66:22] They uh didn't miss the bus
[66:25] We had to get some kid to to the doctor to the dentist
[66:29] We had a work thing that was going on and I was late for that
[66:33] And so now i'm late for my appointment when I get in the parking lot
[66:36] I can't find a parking place and now i'm going to be even later for my appointment like the list goes on and on y'all
[66:41] All of those things are going to raise blood pressure
[66:44] Never trust your office values get people testing their blood pressure at home when they're at rest
[66:50] I never encourage patients to check blood pressure the first couple hours. They're awake. Why?
[66:56] What's happening the first couple hours patients are awake?
[67:11] What's going on those first couple hours you wake up
[67:16] What's going on
[67:20] Resting
[67:22] Elevated cortisol for sure your cortisol is part of what wakes you up your free t3 has been released before you woke up
[67:30] Those two chemicals are raising blood pressure
[67:34] The more alert you become
[67:37] The more demand on your cardiac muscle to fund this whole body with oxygenated blood
[67:44] So there's a huge demand on the heart the first hour ish as you're waking up and getting up
[67:52] So I don't encourage people to check blood pressure early in the day
[67:56] I typically prefer them to check it late in the afternoon after they're done with their work
[68:01] After they can sit down and rest for 30 or 40 minutes and make them lay their arm down heart high
[68:08] At least heart high because if it's down
[68:11] It's dependent that fluid the blood flow is going to come down. It's not going to come up very well
[68:17] So get the arm level with the heart or just above or your best blood pressure reading
[68:23] I have a whole dot phrase macro that I put in my notes
[68:26] For patients that teach them all these things about checking their blood pressure effectively
[68:31] And then track it if you want patients to really begin to understand their blood pressure get them to use graph paper
[68:40] Plot 135 over 85 plot it on a graph number the little lines and watch the dots
[68:47] Numbers are hard for people to pay attention to and is it going up? Is it going down?
[68:53] Which number am I looking at? I don't know
[68:55] Dots are easy to look at you can see the dots come down over time or go up over time or fluctuate over
[69:02] Dots are easy
[69:04] So I really do encourage people to graph their blood pressure
[69:08] Early in this process. I don't have people check it every day like a couple times a week is all they need to check
[69:15] But if we are going to prescribe lio
[69:18] That raises blood pressure. They need to be able to pay attention to their blood pressure
[69:23] It's a safety issue. They need to watch that number
[69:27] Get their waist circumference. We all know that's a marker of metabolic syndrome
[69:32] But the the higher it is the more insulin resistance the more metabolic syndrome we see
[69:38] Get that insulin level down below five because that is crucial to good health
[69:44] any a1c that's above 5.2 is
[69:48] Marker of insulin resistance may be mild
[69:51] They may have an insulin level of 5.2 and an a1c of 5.3. They are still insulin resistant
[69:57] Are they as insulin resistant as someone with an insulin of 23 and an a1c of 5.9?
[70:03] definitely not but
[70:06] A caution flag might be waving rather than a red flag
[70:11] Okay
[70:11] So help patients understand you might not have to give up all the grains to get your a1c and your insulin down
[70:17] but
[70:18] There are reasons besides your glucose and insulin to give up grains
[70:22] And we'll talk about them when we get to your inflammation markers or or whatever
[70:26] Um, so all of those things kind of matter if you are watching fasting glucose numbers
[70:32] and you are
[70:33] Tracking over time if it was 98 last time and now it's 103 and now it's 114
[70:41] That is a trend upwards. That's insulin resistance because they're
[70:45] Not able to keep the glucose down appropriately with the insulin they have on board
[70:51] so
[70:52] Triglycerides greater than about 90
[70:54] indicates some poor management of glucose and insulin
[70:58] So probably a marker of insulin resistance and then an hgl lower than 50 is almost always associated with poor cardiovascular health
[71:06] Okay
[71:09] More ways to look and the reason we draw all these other labs a dha sulfate in the upper or lower third of the range
[71:18] We want dha sulfate to be in the middle of that bell curve
[71:22] Whatever their dha range is we want it to be right in the middle
[71:27] So dha sulfate has varied normal ranges
[71:32] Your teenagers will have a range boys and girls your females childbearing age will have a range
[71:41] Your men will have a range your menopausal women will have a range
[71:44] So make sure when you're looking at your dha you're not just looking at the number. Oh the number looks fine
[71:51] Nope, you need to look at the range
[71:54] Because most childbearing age women range up to about 400 for 20 for 30
[72:01] So for that age group our goal dha sulfate would be about 200 because that'd be right in the middle
[72:08] 200 210
[72:11] Uh for our menopausal women dha sulfate upper limit of the range is about 200 250 give or take depends on the lab
[72:19] you're looking at so for that patient a dha sulfate of
[72:24] 195

[78:34] Um comment
[78:39] Eat rich live long. Yes is the name of the book and it's by gerber and Cummins
[78:47] Kidela says if you restrict carbs over time, do you have less metabolic flexibility? No. Oops. Sorry still no water
[78:53] Um, no, you actually have more metabolic flexibility because when you are eating all carbs
[78:59] or high carbs
[79:01] Your body is going to follow the path of least resistance
[79:05] I haven't shared this part of the story with anybody in a long time. Good question fiela
[79:10] So the path of least resistance imagine a sand pile if I pour water
[79:16] On top of the sand pile
[79:19] Where's that water going to go?
[79:21] It's going to follow the path of least resistance if there's a lump of sand popped up over here on this side of the little mound
[79:29] It's going to go around that the water's going to go. It's not going to fight to go through it or over it
[79:34] It's going to follow the path of least resistance
[79:37] Carbohydrates break down into glucose following the path of least resistance. They're the quickest and easiest things to break down to glucose
[79:44] Okay
[79:46] Proteins will break down into glucose especially in the absence of carbs
[79:51] So somebody that goes keto or low carb carnivore
[79:55] They will quickly transition to burning ketones
[79:59] The cool thing is the body will always follow the path of least resistance
[80:03] So you can be carnivore for 20 years if you start eating fruits again
[80:08] Your body's going to break the fruit down into glucose first always and forever because it's following the path of least resistance
[80:16] Isn't that remarkable?
[80:17] I mean, it's kind of a miracle
[80:20] Because a lot of times this is a myth kiddo
[80:23] What you think is has been a big myth in
[80:26] some of the mainstream and
[80:28] Nutrition world that if you stop eating carbs your body won't have the flexibility to do that
[80:34] But when you think about it physiologically biologically cellularly
[80:41] Molecules are going to follow the path of least resistance
[80:44] Fats will become glucose molecules, but that's a lot of resistance to overcome
[80:49] And so fats will become glucose, but it's a very long drawn out process
[80:55] And so in the presence of carbs that won't happen in the presence of a lot of protein that won't happen
[81:02] So that is why we want to restrict carbs and
[81:06] moderate moderate
[81:08] I don't know what the word would be eat moderate protein amounts so that
[81:15] The fats have to become ketone bodies or glucose molecules that have to be burned
[81:20] The body can burn ketone bodies in place of glucose molecules
[81:25] One fits the other just as easily
[81:27] It's the same as if you put regular gas in your car or premium gas in your car. They both work, right?
[81:33] One works better than the other but the same with proteins and
[81:37] Carves and fats
[81:39] But one works better than the other protein and fats do better nutritionally than carbs, but carbs will work
[81:46] Okay
[81:47] So yeah, very good question
[81:52] So, how do we slow it or stop it well that's what we're doing in this whole course we're learning about optimizing lab values
[81:59] Fixing vitamin d iron protein keep in mind like I mentioned earlier
[82:04] Um, uh when you're talking about whether you're eating enough protein or consume or absorbing enough
[82:10] We have no these labs will not tell us that there's no way we can interpret whether the patient is eating enough protein

[82:23] 150 grams of protein a day they're in taking enough
[82:27] They're not absorbing it if they tell you they're eating. Uh, I had a patient tell me this the other day

[82:35] um, and uh, she

[82:42] Red meat doesn't matter four ounces is a good max for her
[82:46] A plus it may be an egg or two. So she's barely getting enough protein to support her body
[82:53] And so her protein levels continue to run on the low side because she's just not able to get more than that
[82:59] Very often so it is crucial that we help patients understand that nutrients are mostly in our meat
[83:08] And that it is crucial that we absorb that when we eat it
[83:13] And then of course optimize thyroid and optimize reproductive hormones. Remember thyroid is sort of your boss
[83:19] hormone
[83:21] So it directs all the rest and so if thyroid gets out of balance or is low
[83:27] It's not going to manage well

[83:35] That is like an 18 year old that's never had to be a manager never had to do anything
[83:41] And now she's the boss
[83:43] Uh, she's confused. She's lost. She has no no support and she's out there floundering
[83:50] Um, if you optimize that that store and you give her a training manager somebody to guide her and help her
[83:57] And support her she's going to succeed and she's going to do well
[84:01] But if you look at the boss hormone as being like a manager of a store
[84:06] You either have a great one or not. So great one
[84:08] Um, that's what your thyroid is is doing for you
[84:13] This statement was found in a
[84:18] released report by the institute of medicine 21 years ago
[84:23] and lots of us in the low carb world have
[84:26] Quoted it and used it multiple times sharing with providers and patients alike, but there is no carbohydrate need whatsoever
[84:35] none
[84:37] Carbs are not essential to life zinc is essential to life iron is essential to life iodine is essential to life
[84:45] Most of our vitamins and minerals are considered essential carbohydrates are not essential because carbohydrates break down to glucose and glucose
[84:55] Is a molecule that can be broken down from other ingredients like proteins and fats
[85:00] So we never have to consume any carbohydrates
[85:03] And that is why carnivore works so well for a lot of patients because as long as you're getting
[85:08] Protein and fat your body can make any glucose molecule that it needs
[85:15] So, how do you figure out how many carbs that you need

[85:43] list of

[85:51] Yes, a hundred grams of carbs a day is considered lower
[85:55] It really is it's not very
[85:57] Keep in mind that each carbohydrate molecule is going to break down into approximately five glucose points
[86:05] So 50 grams of carbs per day
[86:09] Is going to raise glucose about 250 points now
[86:12] Not all at once because most people don't eat them all at once we spread those out over the day
[86:16] We have breakfast lunch and dinner and two snacks if we're eating like mainstream tells us
[86:21] so
[86:22] um
[86:23] We have 250 molecules of glucose
[86:27] That are going up and down all day long if we're having 50 molecules or 50 grams of carbs per day
[86:33] So low carb then is lower than that
[86:37] So lower low carb would be about 30 to 50 grams of carbs per day
[86:41] Keto generally is classified as about 30 grams of carbs per day
[86:46] Therapeutic keto is 20 grams of carbs a day or less and that is typically where patients see the best results with their help

[96:39] I have a random question that related to this topic. I had a patient come in today who just found out she's pregnant
[96:45] Uh, and she has lots of things that we can work on
[96:48] She's very constipated
[96:50] Our magnesium and vitamin c safe to get her on to start moving her bowels and oh, absolutely
[96:57] Okay, first thing you want to do the the most natural thing is to support water
[97:03] Electrolyte salt and butter like those are the easiest things to make sure
[97:08] Pregnant women just get constipated. They're moving a little bit less often
[97:13] Um, uh, they're they're not as active often like
[97:19] That's not always the case and I know that but like just especially
[97:23] the bigger the baby gets the more challenging it is the baby takes up a lot of room and starts pushing on um
[97:31] The digestive tract. So there's a lot of reasons that contribute to
[97:35] Um poor gut motility in our pregnant women
[97:39] But start with the basic salt water electrolytes and butter and if they aren't increasing them
[97:47] Enough they need to increase them some more
[97:49] So my typical recommendation is make sure you're getting two teaspoons of salt per day
[97:54] All through the day never all at once a big dose of salt water will be a great natural laxative
[98:01] um
[98:02] Butter I typically recommend two tablespoons of butter on every plate of food
[98:08] If people will get two tablespoons of butter on two plates a day two plates
[98:14] That will be about 50 grams of fat
[98:18] And combined with whatever other fats that you're consuming. That's generally an adequate fat intake for the day
[98:24] So it's not a massive amount of fat. It sounds like a lot when you say two tablespoons of butter, but it's 50 ish grams
[98:30] So it's not that much in the whole scheme of things
[98:34] um
[98:35] and then um
[98:38] Um water make sure our she's drinking plenty of water
[98:42] I try to get somewhere between two and three quarts around 80 ounces a day in
[98:48] Um, I try to drink three sand leaves every day
[98:52] Um, and in the summer I typically get four or five
[98:56] Uh, just because it's hot down here
[98:59] Um, you can do keto while pregnant. The baby is always keto
[99:02] The baby is running on ketone bodies
